IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
ACE Louisiana created opportunities to incubate and accelerate 
ideas, get inspired, find new partners, and build long-term 
connections vital for economic growth.
1. Data reflects information extracted from responses to the Final Survey received as of June 2022.
ACE Louisiana had 55 participants. Out of the 55 total participants, 41 responded to the Final Survey, which accounts for 75% of 
the total group of participants. Therefore, when referring to 100% of respondents, it represents the answers provided by the 41 
participants who answered the survey.
The term participants refers to the global and regional leaders who were selected and attended the week-long ACE Louisiana 
program. The term respondents refers to the ACE Louisiana participants who completed the Final Survey. The complete list of 
participants is available on page 70.
The State of Louisiana in the United States successfully implemented the Americas 
Competitiveness Exchange (ACE), resulting in over 200 immediate collaboration 
opportunities that the teams are tracking, monitoring, and facilitating. The week-long 
program took place in New Orleans, Baton Rouge, Lafayette, and Acadiana, Louisiana 
from March 26 to April 1, 2022. Over 100 leaders engaged in and contributed to the ACE 
Louisiana week-long program, including participants, special guests, local organizers, local 
authorities and experts, federal and regional organizations entities, and ACE Committee 
Member institutions authorities and staff. 
100% of the VIP global and regional leaders who responded1 to the survey valued 
the program with high scores and fulfilled their expectations. Out of the total survey 
respondents (equivalent to 41 participants), 89% expressed that ACE Louisiana exceeded 
or greatly exceeded their expectations. (Detailed information about the Final Survey is 
available on page 23).
ACE participants learned about the vast economic opportunities from partnering with 
Southern Louisiana by experiencing Louisiana’s lifestyle and business climate first-hand 
and engaging with the state’s versatile workforce, entrepreneurial ecosystem, innovative 
leaders, world-class research and development facilities, cultural institutions, tourist 
pipelines, and more.
Since its creation, the State of Louisiana has been home to innovators, decision-makers, 
and adventurers from across the globe. Louisianans also mean business. Despite several 
natural and climate disasters hitting the region, Louisianans continue to maintain their 
resolve and push forward to create novel solutions toward a brighter future for all. That’s 
why participants found Louisianans driving innovation within advanced manufacturing, 
port logistics, biosciences, water management, energy, and other increasingly valuable 
sectors. Additionally, it was shared that Louisiana remains a global leader in exports by 
volume and the only state in the U.S. where all six railroads have direct access to major 
ports, which demonstrates their resiliency and status as one of the most attractive places 
to do business.

What is the 
#ACXchange
The 
Americas 
Competitiveness 
Exchange 
(ACE) is the Premier Economic Development, 
Innovation, 
and 
Entrepreneurial 
Network 
of the Americas and is one of the leading 
High–Level programs of the Organization of 
American States (OAS). The ACE Network 
is managed by the OAS as RIAC Technical 
Secretariat, 
with 
the 
support 
from 
the 
Government of the United States, through 
the Department of Commerce [the Economic 
Development Administration (EDA) and the 
International Trade Administration (ITA)], and 
the Department of State/Permanent Mission 
of the U.S. to the OAS.
ACE activities include the Global Recognized 
Week-long Programs, in-person and virtual 
technical assistance meetings, 1-1 virtual 
conversations, ACE Connect Sessions (as part of 
the follow-up mechanism) that boost results, 
cooperation opportunities, and participation 
of ACE in other fora and events. Activities 
aim to support economic recovery, promote 
economic equity, accelerate partnerships, 
expand knowledge and networks, boost job 
creation, 
nurture 
business 
development, 
inspire new projects, strengthen initiatives, 
and empower communities.

IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
The ACE Louisiana program started in New Orleans, continued to Baton Rouge, moved 
to Lafayette and the Acadiana Region and returned to New Orleans. The delegation 
was able to connect with over 30 sites during the one-week program while sharing best 
practices and enhancing collaboration opportunities on innovation, entrepreneurship, 
and economic development.

Lafayette, Acadiana

18
IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
The group moved on to Metal Shark for a tour of the boat manufacturer’s cutting-edge 
facilities and explored first-hand how it has risen as a leader within the ship-building 
industry.
The next stop of the day was Tabasco Factory on Avery Island, 
where the delegation toured the manufacturing facilities 
and learned about how a family-owned business expanded 
globally and stakeholders from the agricultural and fishing 
industries addressed their innovative framework of supply 
chain, costal restoration and resiliency in the fishing industry. 
On the way back to New Orleans, the delegation stopped at the Thibodaux Regional 
Wellness Center for a business showcase.

19
IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
New Orleans 
Friday, April 1, 2022
Watch Summary Video: https://youtu.be/bo3HX8cnbzY
The last day of the program started at Xavier University 
of Louisiana, where interactive panels covered equitable 
development strategies in education and in the 
entrepreneurial community.

20
IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
Later, the group went on a walking tour of Mardi Gras World 
and discussed the cultural economy and strategies for hosting 
major events with stakeholders from the entertainment 
industry of New Orleans. 
To continue the program, the global and regional leaders 
visited the Clairborne Corridor Cultural Innovation District, a 
culture-based economic driver for indigenous entrepreneurs 
& culture bearers. Speakers at the emblematic Dooky Chase 
Restaurant shared community-led goals on equitable & 
sustainable development.
The last official visit of the program was the Port of New 
Orleans, where leaders from multiple ports discussed logistics 
and trade in the Port System of Southern Louisiana. 
To conclude this week-long journey in style, delegates took 
part in the final evening festivities. The Closing Ceremony took 
place in Gallier Hall, where keynote addresses and remarks 
were given by the United States Economic Development 
Administration, the Organization of American States and 
Louisiana Economic Development, among others.

21
IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
A final announcement from the government of Ecuador, 
through the Ministry of Production, Foreign Trade, Investments 
and Fisheries Vice Minister Carla Muirragui, encouraged 
all attendees to continue enhancing inclusive economic 
development , innovation and entrepreneurship through the 
Americas Competitiveness Exchange Network and invited 
participants, authorities, senior leaders to join the next edition 
of ACE in Ecuador in the fall of 2022. 
Certificates of recognition were handed to all ACE Louisiana 
participants for their commitment and dedication to 
promoting inclusive economic development and their 
valuable contributions to the ACE Louisiana week-long 
program. Participants took their official picture with the 
Secretary for Economic Development of the State of 
Louisiana, Secretary Don Pierson.

A NEW VISION 
AT THE CORE 
OF THE 
PROGRAM

25
IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
ACE Louisiana presented an equity-oriented vision at the core of the economic 
development policies. Throughout the program, host sites and speakers highlighted 
the top priority of including underrepresented populations in business development 
and innovation strategies as mandatory actions to achieve prosperous, competitive, 
and sustainable economic growth in any city, region, or country. 
For instance, the Latin American/Hispanic business ownership in New Orleans was 
discussed by the Hispanic Chamber of Commerce of Louisiana (HCCL) and El Centro. 
Mayra Pineda
President and CEO
Hispanic Chamber of Commerce of 
Louisiana (HCCL)

shared with the delegation regarding

education, and entrepreneurial community 
building. The agenda also included a 
showcase of Historically Black Colleges 
and Universities (HBCUs) and an interactive 
panel where female entrepreneurs and 
founders presented their strategies to 
create and expand their businesses and 
valuable tools to scale-up their companies.
Dr. Arthur McMahan, 
Senior Associate Director, 
White House Initiative on HBCUs-
Department of Education
Participants valued the equity-oriented approach presented during ACE Louisiana and 
appreciated the opportunity to explore first-hand economic development projects 
and initiatives that prioritized equity and inclusion of underserved communities 
across industries and sectors. Please read in next page some of the testimonies of 
participants related to this topic.

27
IMPACT REPORT - ACE LOUISIANA
The programs about STEM minorities and gender related were excellent. Fostering STEM fields for girls would 
be an excellent program for my University, which is well known for its inclusive and territorial outreach. 
Based on my participation in ACE, it reaffirms my belief that inclusivity is KEY--to get ALL of our 
creativity on the table to tackle the biggest global challenges. 
The approach to development being utilized in Louisiana is exemplary. 
A lot of lessons were learnt that I will be taking home to apply. 
ACE Louisiana made me realize that I’m an important part of the change we need to create in the system 
to allow inclusion of underrepresented populations in the economic development strategies. Great panels 
about the topic, with different approaches, helped understand the many ways of addressing this problem. 
Our female entrepreneurship acceleration program could be amplified to include other social inclusion 
challenges and afro descendent leaders, for instance.

The Comprehensive Economic Development Strategy (CEDS) of the U.S. Economic 
Development Administration (EDA) was highlighted at ACE Louisiana as a key tool to 
advance equitable and inclusive economic development at the disposal of the ACE 
Network. CEDS provides the capacity-building foundation for the public sector to work 
collaboratively with other economic actors (individuals, firms, industries) to foster 
regional economic prosperity. According to the CEDS, an inclusive process is vital to 
build leadership, enhance cooperation, and enable public ownership and enthusiasm 
(U.S. Economic Development Administration, n.d.).

(Velia Govaere, Head - Center of Observatory of Foreign Trade, Universidad Estatal a Distancia (UNED), Costa Rica)
“Louisiana has a highly integrated innovation and entrepreneurship ecosystem with a strong presence 
by multiple levels of government: local, state, and federal.” 
(Dr. Xavier Ordeñana, Dean of the Business School, ESPOL, Ecuador)
‘Example of resilience and capacity for the economic recovery of the state and its communities’ 
(Ana María Molina, Director, Alliance for Entrepreneurship and Innovation (AEI), Ecuador)
“Louisiana’s ecosystem was built from creating talent, rather than building infrastructure to attract talent.” 
(Carlos Guirola, Minister-Counselor, Embassy of El Salvador in the U.S.)
“Actions to mitigate disaster risks and emergency situations are top priority, and there are valuable 
good practices.” (Julio E. Orozco, Commercial Counselor, Embassy of Guatemala to the United States)

(Dr. Charah T. Watson, Executive Director, Scientific Research Council, Jamaica)
“Louisiana innovation in the medical area, as well as coastal activities is outstanding. The capacity 
and potential of creating, developing and promoting its entrepreneurial ecosystem for their people is 
remarkable.” (Astrid Salazar, Director of Investment Promotion, Tamaulipas State Government, Mexico)
“Coalition between governments, academia, and private sector. There are a lot of public grants and 
incentives.” (Alexander Winer, CEO, Rutas y Raíces (Sustainability consulting firm), Mexico)
“Louisiana is boosting economic development in a variety of sectors, like health, bioscience, culture, 
films to water, and climate change.” 
(Fausto Gurrea, Director Strategic Projects, Mexican Business Council, Mexico)
“Economic development models seek equity and social welfare.” 
(Stephanie García, Deputy Director, Rutas y Raíces, Mexico)

(Roberto Pineda, President, Private Sector Council for Educational Assistance (COSPAE), Panama)

and money spent.” 
(Denise Ferguson, Manager, Caribbean Industrial Research Institute (CARIRI), Trinidad and Tobago)
